sections.heading: Reported intimate partner violence fell nationally from 2022 to 2025
sections.body: Across the country, 15.4 percent of women who ever had a husband or intimate partner reported physical, sexual, or emotional violence in 2025. This was lower than 17.5 percent in 2022. Each main type also declined: emotional violence fell from 15.2 to 14.0 percent, physical violence from 6.4 to 5.3 percent, sexual violence from 2.3 to 1.8 percent, and physical or sexual violence from 7.3 to 5.8 percent. Measures that combine overlapping forms changed less, with physical and sexual violence at 1.3 percent and all three forms together at 1.2 percent in 2025.

sections.heading: Regions differed widely, with Caraga and Bicol among the highest
sections.body: Regional levels in 2025 varied a lot. Physical, sexual, or emotional violence ranged from 24.0 percent in Caraga to 3.2 percent in BARMM. Bicol Region was almost as high as Caraga at 23.9 percent, followed by Zamboanga Peninsula¹ at 21.1 percent, Central Luzon at 20.5 percent, and Eastern Visayas at 20.3 percent. The high-rate regions did not all have the same pattern. Caraga had the highest regional level of emotional violence at 21.8 percent, while Bicol had the highest physical violence level at 9.1 percent and the highest physical or sexual violence level at 10.2 percent.

sections.heading: Some regions improved sharply, while others rose
sections.body: Several regions reported lower levels in 2025 than in 2022, but the change was not the same everywhere. For physical, sexual, or emotional violence, Ilocos Region fell from 25.0 to 12.8 percent, and Central Visayas fell from 18.6 to 9.2 percent. MIMAROPA also declined from 23.7 to 19.5 percent, and Davao Region declined from 20.7 to 16.9 percent. In contrast, Caraga rose from 20.2 to 24.0 percent and Central Luzon rose from 17.0 to 20.5 percent. This shows that the national decline was not shared evenly across regions.

sections.heading: Physical and sexual violence told a different part of the story
sections.body: Regions with high physical, sexual, or emotional violence were often high because of emotional violence. But physical and sexual violence showed another pattern. Davao Region had a lower summary level than Caraga, Bicol, Zamboanga Peninsula, Central Luzon, Eastern Visayas, MIMAROPA, and Western Visayas, yet it had one of the highest 2025 levels of physical or sexual violence at 9.3 percent. Western Visayas was also high on physical or sexual violence at 8.6 percent, with sexual violence at 3.1 percent. This means regional risk cannot be understood from the summary measure alone.

sections.heading: BARMM had the lowest regional levels across the measures
sections.body: BARMM stood out as the lowest region in 2025, with 3.2 percent reporting physical, sexual, or emotional violence. Its separate measures were also the lowest or among the lowest: 2.9 percent for emotional violence, 0.7 percent for physical violence, 0.4 percent for sexual violence, and 0.9 percent for physical or sexual violence. BARMM also declined from 5.6 percent in 2022 on the summary measure. However, the data alone do not explain why its profile is so different from the rest of the country.

sections.heading: Age differences were present but not large
sections.body: Across age groups, 2025 physical, sexual, or emotional violence stayed in a fairly narrow range. It went from 14.3 percent among women aged 20 to 24 to 16.1 percent among women aged 40 to 49. Women aged 25 to 29 were close behind at 16.0 percent, and adolescents aged 15 to 19 were at 15.4 percent. Physical violence rose from 3.3 percent among ages 15 to 19 to 6.3 percent among ages 25 to 29, then stayed near 5 percent in the older age groups. Age differences were visible, but smaller than the regional, marital status, and wealth divides.

sections.heading: Religion groups differed, but some high values came from small samples
sections.body: Among religion categories with 2025 values, Islam had the lowest level of physical, sexual, or emotional violence at 7.1 percent. Roman Catholic women were at 15.6 percent, Protestant women at 17.1 percent, and Iglesia ni Cristo women at 14.7 percent. Aglipay was high at 23.1 percent, driven especially by physical violence at 13.7 percent. The Other category reached 24.3 percent, almost entirely because emotional violence was 24.2 percent. Because Aglipay and Other had much smaller 2025 sample counts than the largest religion categories, those higher values should be read more cautiously than the broad national pattern.

sections.heading: Bikolano and Waray women had high unflagged levels among ethnic groups
sections.body: Among ethnic groups, Bikolano and Waray women had the highest unflagged 2025 levels of physical, sexual, or emotional violence, at 22.2 and 21.4 percent. For both groups, the main driver was emotional violence, at 20.0 percent in each group. Bikolano women also had comparatively high physical violence at 8.4 percent and physical or sexual violence at 9.1 percent. Maranao women had the lowest unflagged summary level at 5.7 percent. Aeta values were higher but flagged in the display data, so they should not be treated as a stable ranking point.

sections.heading: The rural-urban gap became smaller, but rural women were still higher on physical and sexual violence
sections.body: In 2025, rural women reported 15.9 percent physical, sexual, or emotional violence, compared with 15.0 percent among urban women. This gap was much smaller than in 2022, when the values were 19.9 and 15.6 percent. Rural women still had higher 2025 levels of physical violence at 5.8 percent versus 4.9 percent, sexual violence at 2.2 percent versus 1.5 percent, and physical or sexual violence at 6.4 percent versus 5.3 percent. Emotional violence was closer, at 14.3 percent in rural areas and 13.8 percent in urban areas.

sections.heading: Marital status showed the largest gap in this page's data
sections.body: Divorced, separated, or widowed women had by far the highest 2025 levels. Among them, 41.4 percent reported physical, sexual, or emotional violence, 39.1 percent reported emotional violence, 17.3 percent physical violence, 6.7 percent sexual violence, and 18.3 percent physical or sexual violence. Women who had an intimate partner but did not currently have one were also high on the summary measure at 33.4 percent. By comparison, married or living-together women were at 13.9 percent, never-married women at 15.7 percent, and women who currently had an intimate partner at 7.4 percent. The marital status pattern is much sharper than the age or residence pattern.

sections.heading: Employment groups looked more alike in 2025
sections.body: Employment groups were more similar in 2025 than in 2022. Physical, sexual, or emotional violence was 15.4 percent among women employed for cash, 15.2 percent among women employed not for cash, and 15.5 percent among women not employed. This was a notable change from 2022, when women employed not for cash had the highest level at 22.1 percent. Some separate measures still differed in 2025: physical or sexual violence was 6.3 percent among women employed for cash, 5.5 percent among women not employed, and 4.4 percent among women employed not for cash.

sections.heading: More schooling was generally linked to lower reported violence, but the pattern was not simple
sections.body: Women with college education had the lowest 2025 level of physical, sexual, or emotional violence by education group at 11.4 percent, with physical or sexual violence at 3.2 percent. Women with Grades 1 to 6 education were highest on the summary measure at 20.2 percent, followed by Grades 11 to 12 at 19.1 percent and Grades 7 to 10 at 17.7 percent. The no-education group had 12.7 percent on the summary measure but much higher physical violence at 9.4 percent and sexual violence at 4.3 percent, based on a smaller sample count. Overall, higher schooling, especially college, was linked with lower reported violence, but the middle education categories did not decline step by step.

sections.heading: Reported violence was lower in wealthier groups
sections.body: Wealth showed one of the clearest patterns in the data. In 2025, physical, sexual, or emotional violence was 19.1 percent in the lowest wealth quintile, 16.5 percent in the second, 15.7 percent in the middle, 15.4 percent in the fourth, and 10.6 percent in the highest. Physical or sexual violence also declined from 8.2 percent in the lowest quintile to 2.8 percent in the highest, and physical violence declined from 7.4 to 2.4 percent. Every wealth quintile was lower on the summary measure than in 2022, but the 2025 gap between the lowest and highest quintiles remained substantial.

takeaways: Reported intimate partner violence fell nationally from 2022 to 2025, but 15.4 percent of women who ever had a husband or intimate partner still reported physical, sexual, or emotional violence.
Regional differences were large: Caraga and Bicol Region were near one-quarter on the summary measure, while BARMM was far lower at 3.2 percent.
Marital status showed the sharpest divide, with divorced, separated, or widowed women reporting much higher violence than other marital status groups.
Wealth and education patterns were clear: the highest wealth quintile and college-educated women had the lowest levels in their respective groups.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
